metadata.title: Music tempo modulates emotional states as revealed through EEG insights
metadata.note: Original EEG study examining effects of music tempo (56, 106, 156 bpm) on emotional states (valence, arousal) and brain functional networks in 26 participants using classical piano music.

claims.claim_text: Valence level significantly increased with rising music tempo (F=222.27, p<0.001, Tukey test), indicating that faster tempo evokes more positive emotional experiences

axis_proposal.definition: Pleasure-displeasure dimension of emotional state, measured on 7-point Likert scale (1=very negative, 7=very positive)
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: Self-reported emotional valence ratings in response to musical stimuli

statistics.note: Valence significantly increased with rising music tempo

brain_regions.context: Emotional regulation and expression, executive control during musical activities. Channels: Fp1, Fp2, AF3, AF4, AF7, AF8, Fpz
Auditory processing and music perception. Channels: T7, T8

methodology.task: Listening to classical piano music at three different tempi (56, 106, 156 bpm)
methodology.participant_characteristics: Right-handed, native Chinese, no history of neurological or psychiatric disorders, no musical training, mean age 23.2 years (range 17-38), 13 females

claims.claim_text: Arousal level exhibited a 'V' shape relationship with music tempo, with the lowest arousal at 106 bpm (1.88±0.54), followed by 56 bpm (3.52±0.78), and the highest at 156 bpm (6.62±0.50) (F=330.89, p<0.001, Tukey test)

axis_proposal.definition: Arousal-nonarousal dimension of emotional state, measured on 7-point Likert scale (1=low arousal, 7=high arousal)
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: Self-reported emotional arousal ratings in response to musical stimuli

statistics.note: Arousal-tempo relationship exhibited 'V' shape, with lowest arousal at medium tempo (106 bpm)

brain_regions.context: Emotional regulation and cognitive control. Channels: Fp1, Fp2, AF3, AF4, AF7, AF8, Fpz

methodology.task: Listening to classical piano music at three different tempi (56, 106, 156 bpm)
methodology.participant_characteristics: Right-handed, native Chinese, no history of neurological or psychiatric disorders, no musical training, mean age 23.2 years (range 17-38), 13 females

claims.claim_text: Slow tempo (56 bpm) induced higher Theta (4-8 Hz) and Alpha (8-12 Hz) power in the frontal region, while fast tempo (156 bpm) increased Beta (12-32 Hz) and Gamma (32-45 Hz) band power

statistics.note: Frontal region: slow tempo highest theta power (154.02±9.76), alpha power significantly decreased with tempo increase (P<0.001), beta power significantly increased with higher tempo (P<0.001)

brain_regions.context: Power spectrum analysis. Channels: Fp1, Fp2, AF3, AF4, AF7, AF8, Fpz. Slow tempo: highest theta (154.02±9.76) and alpha power. Fast tempo: highest beta and gamma power
Delta band power highest at medium tempo (407.22±14.19). Channels: P5, P6, P3, P4, P1, P2, Pz
Theta power significantly decreased as tempo increased (P<0.05), slow tempo highest theta power (21.43±2.47). Channels: T7, T8
Theta power significantly decreased as tempo increased (P<0.05), slow tempo highest theta power (24.65±2.95). Channels: PO3, PO4, PO7, PO8, O1, O2, Oz

methodology.task: Listening to classical piano music at three different tempi (56, 106, 156 bpm)
methodology.participant_characteristics: Right-handed, native Chinese, no history of neurological or psychiatric disorders, no musical training, mean age 23.2 years (range 17-38), 13 females
methodology.analysis_details: Power spectrum analysis with window lengths of 0.5s with 0.25s overlap. Frequency bands: delta (1-4 Hz), theta (4-8 Hz), alpha (8-12 Hz), beta (12-32 Hz), gamma (32-45 Hz)

claims.claim_text: Fast tempo (156 bpm) enhanced the average connectivity strength in the frontal (21.91±2.55), temporal (21.40±2.80), and occipital (14.94±1.95) regions compared to slow tempo (frontal: 19.35±2.85, temporal: 16.30±3.31, occipital: 10.61±2.27) (frontal: P<0.05, temporal: P<0.001, occipital: P<0.001, Tukey test)

axis_proposal.definition: Average node strength in brain network based on covariance (COV) analysis, measuring the importance or centrality of nodes within weighted network
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: Covariance-based connectivity strength between EEG channels, calculated as sum of connection weights from a node

statistics.note: Fast tempo significantly increased connectivity strength in frontal, temporal, and occipital regions

brain_regions.context: Fast tempo: 21.91±2.55, slow tempo: 19.35±2.85 (P<0.05). Channels: Fp1, Fp2, AF3, AF4, AF7, AF8, Fpz
Fast tempo: 21.40±2.80, slow tempo: 16.30±3.31 (P<0.001). T8 channel connectivity strength increased with faster tempo (P<0.001), T7 channel decreased (P<0.001). Channels: T7, T8
Fast tempo: 14.94±1.95, slow tempo: 10.61±2.27 (P<0.001). Channels: PO3, PO4, PO7, PO8, O1, O2, Oz
FCz channel node strength significantly increased at 156 bpm compared to both 56 bpm (p<0.01) and 106 bpm (p<0.01), reflecting increased auditory-motor synchronization and cognitive load

methodology.task: Listening to classical piano music at three different tempi (56, 106, 156 bpm)
methodology.participant_characteristics: Right-handed, native Chinese, no history of neurological or psychiatric disorders, no musical training, mean age 23.2 years (range 17-38), 13 females
methodology.analysis_details: Covariance (COV) analysis examining connectivity among 59 channels. Node strength calculated as sum of connection weights from a node. Clustering coefficient and degree distribution also analyzed

claims.claim_text: Fast tempo (156 bpm) significantly increased phase locking value (PLV) between the frontal and parietal regions (PLV=0.76, P<0.01, Tukey test), while slow tempo (56 bpm) improved PLV between the occipital and parietal regions

axis_proposal.definition: Phase locking value measuring phase synchronization between brain regions, computed as absolute value of average of complex phase differences over time
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: PLV quantifies consistency of phase differences between two EEG signals over time, with high PLV indicating strong phase synchrony or coupling between brain areas

statistics.note: Fast tempo: frontal-parietal PLV=0.76 (P<0.01). Slow tempo: occipital-parietal synchronization. Medium tempo: central-temporal PLV=0.69 (P<0.01)

brain_regions.context: Highest PLV at 156 bpm. Fast tempo enhanced synchronization with parietal region (PLV=0.76, P<0.01). Channels: Fp1, Fp2, AF3, AF4, AF7, AF8, Fpz
Peak PLV values at 156 bpm. Fast tempo: increased synchronization with frontal region. Slow tempo: increased synchronization with occipital region. Channels: P5, P6, P3, P4, P1, P2, Pz
Highest PLV at 106 bpm. Medium tempo: strengthened synchronization with central region (PLV=0.69, P<0.01). Fast tempo: marked synchronization with frontal region (PLV=0.76). Channels: T7, T8
Peak PLV values at 156 bpm. Slow tempo: higher synchronization with parietal region. Fast tempo: increased synchronization with temporal region (PLV=0.93). Channels: PO3, PO4, PO7, PO8, O1, O2, Oz

methodology.task: Listening to classical piano music at three different tempi (56, 106, 156 bpm)
methodology.participant_characteristics: Right-handed, native Chinese, no history of neurological or psychiatric disorders, no musical training, mean age 23.2 years (range 17-38), 13 females
methodology.analysis_details: PLV computed as absolute value of average of complex phase differences over time. Phase extraction using Hilbert transform or wavelet transform. PLV measures phase synchronization between brain regions

c3_associations.process: Music tempo modulates sensorimotor timing and rhythm processing, with fast tempo requiring higher cognitive load for tempo tracking and auditory-motor synchronization

brain_regions.role: Right superior temporal cortex responsible for processing musical tempo

c3_associations.evidence: Fast tempo enhanced FCz node strength (p<0.01), reflecting increased auditory-motor synchronization and cognitive load for rhythm tracking. Right temporal lobe (T8) showed increased connectivity with faster tempo, while left temporal lobe (T7) decreased, suggesting right hemisphere dominance in tempo processing.

c3_associations.process: Music tempo significantly modulates emotional valence and arousal, with fast tempo evoking positive valence and high arousal, while medium tempo (106 bpm) elicits lowest arousal levels

brain_regions.role: Emotional regulation and expression, executive control during musical activities

c3_associations.evidence: Valence significantly increased with rising tempo (F=222.27, p<0.001). Arousal exhibited 'V' shape: lowest at 106 bpm (1.88±0.54), highest at 156 bpm (6.62±0.50). Fast tempo increased PLV between frontal and parietal regions (PLV=0.76, P<0.01), supporting enhanced emotional and cognitive arousal.

c3_associations.process: Music tempo modulates EEG power spectral density across frequency bands, with slow tempo inducing higher theta and alpha power (relaxation), and fast tempo increasing beta and gamma power (heightened neural activation)

brain_regions.role: Slow tempo: highest theta (154.02±9.76) and alpha power. Fast tempo: highest beta and gamma power
Theta power decreased with tempo increase (P<0.05), slow tempo highest (21.43±2.47)
Theta power decreased with tempo increase (P<0.05), slow tempo highest (24.65±2.95)

c3_associations.evidence: Power spectrum analysis revealed significant association between brain activity across frequency bands and music tempo. Frontal region: slow tempo highest theta and alpha power (relaxation), fast tempo highest beta and gamma power (heightened activation). Temporal and occipital regions: theta power significantly decreased with tempo increase.

c3_associations.process: Music tempo modulates brain functional connectivity and phase synchronization, with fast tempo enhancing connectivity strength and frontal-parietal synchronization, while slow tempo promotes occipital-parietal synchronization

brain_regions.role: Fast tempo: enhanced connectivity strength (21.91±2.55 vs 19.35±2.85, P<0.05) and increased PLV with parietal region (0.76, P<0.01)
Fast tempo: enhanced connectivity strength (21.40±2.80 vs 16.30±3.31, P<0.001). Right temporal (T8) increased, left temporal (T7) decreased with faster tempo
Fast tempo: increased PLV with frontal region. Slow tempo: increased PLV with occipital region
Fast tempo: enhanced connectivity strength (14.94±1.95 vs 10.61±2.27, P<0.001). Slow tempo: increased PLV with parietal region

c3_associations.evidence: Covariance (COV) analysis: fast tempo enhanced average connectivity strength in frontal, temporal, and occipital regions. PLV analysis: fast tempo increased phase synchronization between frontal and parietal regions (PLV=0.76, P<0.01), while slow tempo improved PLV between occipital and parietal regions.

primary_axes.note: Three tempo conditions: slow (56 bpm), medium (106 bpm), fast (156 bpm)
EEG frequency bands: delta (1-4 Hz, center 2.5 Hz), theta (4-8 Hz, center 6 Hz), alpha (8-12 Hz, center 10 Hz), beta (12-32 Hz, center 22 Hz), gamma (32-45 Hz, center 38.5 Hz)

r3_coordinates.note: Tempo (L6.tempo_bpm) is the primary independent variable. EEG power spectral density across frequency bands (L4.rate_hz) and connectivity measures (PLV, COV) are dependent variables. Medium tempo (106 bpm) matches optimal human auditory experience window (85-120 bpm) according to 'Medium Tempo Attraction Theory', explaining lowest arousal at this tempo.

Medium tempo: strengthened synchronization with central region (PLV=0.69, P<0.01). Fast tempo: marked synchronization with frontal region (PLV=0.76)

Fast tempo: enhanced connectivity strength. Right temporal (T8) increased, left temporal (T7) decreased
Delta band power highest at medium tempo (407.22±14.19)
Fast tempo: increased PLV with frontal region. Slow tempo: increased PLV with occipital region

Theta power decreased with tempo increase (P<0.05), slow tempo highest (24.65±2.95)
Fast tempo: enhanced connectivity strength (14.94±1.95 vs 10.61±2.27, P<0.001)

FCz channel node strength significantly increased at 156 bpm compared to both 56 bpm (p<0.01) and 106 bpm (p<0.01), reflecting increased auditory-motor synchronization and cognitive load
Auditory-motor synchronization and rhythm perception

brain_regions_summary.note: All brain regions identified through EEG electrode placement in 10-10 international standard system. Frontal, temporal, parietal, and occipital regions analyzed. Specific channels mentioned: Fp1, Fp2, AF3, AF4, AF7, AF8, Fpz (frontal); T7, T8 (temporal); P5, P6, P3, P4, P1, P2, Pz (parietal); PO3, PO4, PO7, PO8, O1, O2, Oz (occipital); FCz (frontal-central). Right temporal (T8) showed increased connectivity with faster tempo, while left temporal (T7) decreased, suggesting right hemisphere dominance in tempo processing.

warnings: 4 DV r3_axis unmapped (axis_proposal provided for all)
EEG electrode-based brain region identification (no MNI coordinates or precise Brodmann area confirmation)

data_quality.note: Original EEG study with 26 participants. All statistics reported (ANOVA F-values, p-values, post-hoc tests). Brain regions identified through EEG electrode placement. R³ coordinates mapped for tempo (L6.tempo_bpm) and frequency bands (L4.rate_hz). Effect sizes not reported but could be calculated from provided means and SDs.
